lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market distributors and coordinators that repeatedly provide branded-merchandise and advertising-specialty programs to external customers, including product sourcing, decoration coordination, creative support, online stores, order management, fulfillment coordination, and account service.
lens.excluded_activities: One-person sales representatives without transferable operations; captive marketing units; shells; non-control financing vehicles; merchandise-demonstration staffing; sign and display lettering; window or mannequin dressing; welcoming services; manufacturers; printers; and full-service advertising agencies classified elsewhere.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and institutional customers buy decorated merchandise and continuing program support. Providers primarily purchase products and branding from third parties and resell finished goods, while some also charge for technology, creative, loyalty, event, warehousing, fulfillment, and related services. Revenue ranges from repeat orders to multi-year, auto-renewing programs, often without minimum purchase guarantees.
lens.deviation_from_default: The official product list combines branded-merchandise coordination, public product demonstrations, lettering, window dressing, and other miscellaneous advertising services. Their labor, delivery, and pricing models are too different for one coherent screen, so the lens is narrowed to recurring branded-merchandise distributor and coordinator services for coherence rather than attractiveness.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across the broader advertising sector and PPAI reports AI uses among leading promotional-products firms, not a wage-weighted task census of eligible LMM distributors. I map product search, quoting, artwork support, order entry, CRM updates, marketing content, routine customer communication, reporting, sourcing comparisons, and administrative coordination to exposure, while retaining relationship selling, complex program design, physical quality checks, kitting, warehousing, and exception handling as less exposed. The proxy may overstate exposure in fulfillment-heavy firms and understate it in digitally mature distributors.
a.rationale: Sales, management, creative, and office work are prominent in advertising-sector employment, and the distributor workflow contains substantial digital information handling. Industry evidence identifies quoting, product recommendations, CRM prioritization, artwork, sourcing, reorders, customer service, and compliance documentation as practical AI opportunities. Existing e-commerce, ERP, and current AI use remove part of the gross opportunity, leaving a material but submajority share of current wage tasks exposed.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study across LMM distributors, joined to payroll and separating already automated e-commerce and ERP work, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The BLS occupation mix is for the broader advertising sector rather than this narrowed industry population.
PPAI technology surveys emphasize leading firms and may not represent LMM operators.
Labor mix varies with in-house warehousing, decoration, creative work, and independent sales representatives.

rho.bridge: PPAI surveys report current uses, ambitions, and barriers among leading suppliers and distributors, while Stran describes technology deployment at one public operator. Neither measures the five-year implemented share of exposed LMM labor. I translate evidence of current marketing, sales, CRM, customer-service, and operations use into implementation potential, discounted for weak data, system integration, privacy, expertise, and change constraints. The proxy may be high for small firms with fragmented systems and low for firms adopting integrated industry platforms.
rho.rationale: The use cases are operationally ordinary and can be added to existing CRM, e-commerce, artwork, and order-management systems. Industry firms are already using AI, yet reported workforce effects remain limited and internal expertise and systems integration are major barriers. Implementation should therefore be meaningful but incomplete, with human review preserved for brand accuracy, landed cost, product compliance, and customer commitments.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor telemetry and audited before-and-after labor records from LMM distributors using integrated AI for quote-to-order, artwork, CRM, and support workflows would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Engagement with AI is not the same as production implementation or labor realization.
Survey respondents are disproportionately prominent industry firms.
Supplier catalog quality and disconnected legacy systems can constrain end-to-end automation.

e.rationale: Branded-merchandise coordination is an explicit product within the code and the trade market is large and fragmented, but the official scope also includes unrelated physical and staffing services. Some distributors are classified in wholesale, printing, or advertising-agency codes, and many tiny representatives lack transferable operations. No source located measures the share of supplied LMM firms satisfying the narrowed lens, so this is a bounded judgment.
e.change_evidence: A business-register match of firms in this NAICS to PPAI or ASI distributor rosters, with normalized EBITDA, external-customer revenue, repeat-order share, and operational headcount, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: This is not an observed eligible-firm share.
The supplied firm count spans every miscellaneous service in the code rather than the narrowed lens.
The supplied margin bridge may overstate LMM eligibility for product resellers with EBITDA margins below the broad business-services assumption.
Industry association market totals do not map cleanly to one NAICS code.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year transfer plans among US employer-business owners generally, while PPAI and ASI document promotional-products acquisitions without an eligible-firm denominator. I anchor the estimate near general employer-owner intent and move the base upward for visible consolidation, fragmentation, strategic buyers, and a public consolidator actively targeting small branded-merchandise firms. I discount for plans that do not close and for portfolio purchases that may not transfer a complete operating company. This may overstate qualifying controls if customer books are not durable and understate them if consolidation accelerates.
s5.rationale: The industry has frequent strategic and private-equity-backed transactions, including small distributor acquisitions, and one public operator reports repeated asset acquisitions and continued target search. Customer portfolios are often the central transferable asset. However, many reported deals are portfolio asset purchases, and owner intentions are not completed control transfers, so the estimate remains moderate rather than treating deal headlines as a probability.
s5.change_evidence: A closed-deal database from Certified Marketing Consultants, ASI, PPAI, GF Data, or PitchBook matched to an eligible US LMM distributor denominator and coded for operating-company control would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry intention evidence rather than closed promotional-products deals.
Trade publications report visible transactions but not a complete denominator.
A customer-portfolio purchase may not qualify if no transferable operation accompanies it.
Independent representative and franchise structures complicate the unit of control.

q.bridge: Stran discloses reseller transaction mechanics, observable order prices, program agreements, gross margins, and competitive pricing risks for a public distributor, while PPAI reports margin pressure across distributor sizes. Neither source measures the retained share of AI benefit for LMM firms. I apply the observed product-resale and service-fee model: internal SG&A savings can initially be retained, but transparent product markets, order rebids, customer allowances, and price competition share gains quickly. The proxy may overstate retention for commoditized orders and understate it for embedded programs with differentiated service.
q.rationale: Most revenue is tied to merchandise with clearly identified selling prices, while program contracts add service and relationship value. AI savings in selling, artwork, support, and administration do not automatically reduce supplier product cost, giving an initial margin channel. Yet buyers compare products and prices, many purchases are order-specific, and competitors can reprice, so only part of the implemented gross benefit is likely to remain with the operator over time.
q.change_evidence: An anonymized panel of LMM distributor quotes, contract renewals, rebates, gross margins, service fees, and cost-to-serve before and after AI adoption would materially change this estimate.
caveats: A public consolidator may have different purchasing power and enterprise-client bargaining dynamics from LMM firms.
Gross-margin disclosure does not isolate retention of AI-created benefit.
Retention differs sharply between transactional merchandise orders and integrated program clients.

d5.bridge: PPAI measures nominal US distributor sales and channel mix, while Stran reports one operator's current demand and contract conditions. I use recent modest nominal growth, broad participation across firm sizes, online-channel gains, and continued corporate program use as evidence for a broadly stable service basket, then remove optimism because recent growth trailed inflation and advertising budgets, tariffs, employment, and events are cyclical. This may overstate constant-price demand if clients shift spend to digital media and understate it if premium branded merchandise and employee programs expand.
d5.rationale: The market remained large and grew modestly in nominal terms despite severe cost and budget pressure, while online sales outpaced the broader channel. Physical merchandise continues to serve campaigns, events, recognition, recruiting, and loyalty programs, but it is often a discretionary secondary budget item. The base therefore implies near-flat to modest real growth rather than extrapolating nominal market expansion.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price, same-service sales panel from PPAI or ASI segmented by distributor size, client use case, order volume, and repeat program revenue would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Trade-association sales estimates are broader than this NAICS and may include firms classified elsewhere.
Nominal sales mix product cost, unit volume, premiumization, and service demand.
Client budgets are sensitive to tariffs, economic conditions, employment, events, and difficulty proving marketing return.

o.bridge: PPAI reports online-channel penetration and AI-suitable workflows, and Stran describes the services performed by a technology-enabled distributor. These sources do not measure the year-five share of demand still requiring an accountable LMM operator. I treat simple product discovery, quoting, artwork preparation, reorders, routine communication, and direct ordering as vulnerable to software or self-service, while retaining supplier selection, brand governance, complex customization, quality, landed-cost management, warehousing, fulfillment, compliance, and client accountability. The proxy may be high for simple commodity orders and low for complex enterprise programs.
o.rationale: E-commerce already handles a meaningful share of sales, and AI can remove friction across common order steps. Customers can also source some goods from general marketplaces or suppliers outside the traditional channel. However, physical products must still be selected, decorated, quality-checked, transported, and delivered against brand, deadline, inventory, and compliance requirements. Integrated programs therefore retain a substantial operator role even as transactional work migrates to software.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement data measuring spend moved from distributors to direct suppliers, marketplaces, software-only platforms, and autonomous fulfillment, segmented by order complexity and program status, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Online sales through a distributor still require an operator and are not equivalent to elimination.
Self-service risk differs greatly between commodity reorders and complex branded programs.
Future supplier platforms may absorb more distributor coordination than current evidence shows.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent population is recurring branded-merchandise distributors and program coordinators, not the full miscellaneous advertising code. Their workflows contain substantial AI-addressable sales, artwork, quoting, service, and administrative work, but competitive product pricing limits retention and physical fulfillment preserves operator need. Eligibility under the supplied firm count is the largest unresolved issue.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate product discovery, quoting, sourcing comparisons, artwork support, CRM prioritization, reorders, routine communication, marketing content, support, and compliance documentation. Existing e-commerce and ERP systems provide integration points. Human work remains important for relationship selling, creative judgment, complex customization, brand control, supplier exceptions, quality, and deadline accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Savings in selling and administration can initially improve margin because merchandise is sold at an agreed order price. Over time, transparent catalogs, frequent rebids, customer allowances, and intense competition share the benefit. Embedded program clients with technology, inventory, creative, and fulfillment support offer better retention than commodity transactions.
narrative.firm_availability: The distributor market is fragmented and visibly consolidating, with strategic and financial buyers active and customer portfolios treated as valuable assets. Still, many deals are asset or portfolio purchases rather than transfers of complete operations. The official code also contains unrelated services, and the supplied margin bridge may misclassify low-margin resellers as LMM firms.
narrative.demand_durability: Branded merchandise supports events, employee engagement, recognition, loyalty, recruiting, and campaigns, while online ordering continues to expand. Recent nominal market growth was modest and did not demonstrate real expansion. Demand remains exposed to discretionary client budgets, tariffs, freight, employment, event activity, product trends, and competition from digital advertising.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: A poor outcome could arise if most apparent targets are unrelated code occupants, low-margin resellers, owner-dependent books, or franchises without transferable operations. Other risks include client concentration, weak purchase guarantees, supplier and tariff volatility, price competition, artwork or product errors, compliance failures, and direct-to-customer platforms. Direct LMM evidence is weak for task weights, implementation, retained benefit, and qualifying transfer probability.
narrative.principal_driver: Information-heavy quote-to-order and account workflows offer practical AI leverage while complex physical branded programs still need an accountable coordinator.
narrative.principal_weakness: Transparent product pricing and uncertain target eligibility can erode both benefit retention and the usable firm pool.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 541890 Other Services Related to Advertising
sources.supports: Defines the miscellaneous industry and identifies advertising-specialty distribution, sign and display lettering, window dressing, mannequin decorating, welcoming, and merchandise demonstration as examples.

sources.title: Census NAPCS Product List for NAICS 54189: Other Services Related to Advertising
sources.supports: Separates coordination of advertising-specialty production and delivery from merchandise demonstrations, display lettering, and other miscellaneous services, supporting the heterogeneity finding and describing the retained lens.

sources.title: BLS OEWS: Advertising, Public Relations, and Related Services
sources.supports: Provides the broader advertising-sector occupational mix, including management, sales, creative, administrative, production, and material-moving work.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Advertising, Promotions, and Marketing Managers
sources.supports: Describes campaign planning, contracts, budgets, research, pricing, client strategy, creative review, and staff direction tasks.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Advertising Sales Agents
sources.supports: Describes prospecting, client needs analysis, proposals, cost estimates, account administration, reporting, proofs, contracts, and relationship work.

sources.title: PPAI Sales Volume Estimate: Promo Reaches New Height But Hampered By External Volatility
sources.supports: Reports current US distributor sales, firm-size participation, online-channel share and growth, margin pressure, non-industry sourcing, and uneven AI adoption focused on CRM, customer insight, and operational automation.

sources.title: PPAI: Promo's AI Challenges and Opportunities
sources.supports: Reports AI uses and ambitions in marketing, sales, service, and operations, along with barriers from internal expertise, integration, privacy, and employee resistance and limited observed workforce change.

sources.title: Stran & Company, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Documents the reseller and program business model, recurring and transactional clients, e-commerce and ERP workflows, supplier and fulfillment coordination, contract and pricing mechanics, competition, fragmentation, acquisitions, margins, and demand risks.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports five-year sale and transfer intentions among US employer-business owners and provides survey scope and methodology.

sources.title: PPAI: ScreenBroidery's M&A Strategy—Portfolios, Not Competitors
sources.supports: Describes small distributor portfolio acquisitions and industry-adviser evidence that customer portfolios, often acquired through asset purchases, are central to many promotional-products transactions.

sources.title: ASI Media: This Year Has Been the Most Momentous Ever for Promo M&A
sources.supports: Documents active promotional-products consolidation involving private equity, major distributors, smaller domestic firms, and asset acquisitions.
